title: Weak Polar Optical Phonon Scattering Decouples Electron and Phonon Transport in Layered Thermoelectric Materials
authors: Zhonghao Xia, Michele Reticcioli, Yateng Wang, Yali Yang, Alessandro Stroppa, Jiangang He
abstract: High-performance thermoelectric (TE) materials are crucial for efficient waste-heat recovery and solid-state cooling technologies. A persistent challenge in TE materials design arises from the strong interdependence among the electrical conductivity ($σ$), Seebeck coefficient ($S$), and lattice thermal conductivity ($κ_{\mathrm{L}}$). Layered compounds can effectively suppress $κ_{\mathrm{L}}$ along the cross-plane direction owing to weak interlayer interactions; however, they often suffer from low carrier mobility ($μ$) caused by limited band dispersion and strong polar optical phonon (POP) scattering. Here, we perform high-throughput density functional theory calculations to screen 236 layered semiconductors and identify candidates with low effective mass ($m^{*}$) and weak POP scattering. We identify 23 compounds with high cross-plane $μ$, among which 14 exhibit large power factors ($S^{2}σ$). Notably, GaGe$_{2}$Te stands out with exceptionally high cross-plane $σ$ and power factor, enabled by a favorable combination of small $m^{*}$ and a small ionic dielectric constant. Simultaneously, GaGe$_{2}$Te exhibits an ultralow cross-plane $κ_{\mathrm{L}}$ of 0.57~W~m$^{-1}$~K$^{-1}$ at 300~K, originating from weak interlayer bonding and pronounced phonon anharmonicity. These results demonstrate an effective strategy to decouple electron and phonon transport in layered materials by mitigating POP scattering, thereby providing a promising pathway toward high-performance thermoelectric materials.
summary: This paper presents a computational strategy to design high-efficiency thermoelectric materials by breaking the link between electron and phonon transport. By screening hundreds of layered semiconductors, the authors found that minimizing the ionic dielectric constant can suppress polar optical phonon scattering, allowing for high carrier mobility. They identify GaGe2Te as a premier candidate that achieves both high electrical power factors and ultralow thermal conductivity due to its unique covalent bonding and structural anisotropy. This 'covalency engineering' approach provides a new pathway for discovering high-performance energy-harvesting materials.
main_problem: The fundamental challenge in designing high-performance thermoelectric materials is the strong interdependence between electrical conductivity, Seebeck coefficient, and lattice thermal conductivity, particularly the trade-off between high mobility and low thermal conductivity in layered compounds.
main_result: The study identifies a strategy to decouple electron and phonon transport by suppressing polar optical phonon (POP) scattering through high covalency, highlighting GaGe2Te as a standout candidate with exceptionally high cross-plane power factor and ultralow thermal conductivity.
method: High-throughput density functional theory (DFT) calculations using VASP and HSE06, combined with Boltzmann transport theory (BoltzTraP2, AMSET, Perturbo) and advanced phonon dynamics (FourPhonon, SCPH, CSLD).
model_or_system: A high-throughput screening of 236 thermodynamically stable layered semiconductors, with a detailed focus on the layered van der Waals semiconductor GaGe2Te, which features a Ge bilayer and strong intralayer covalent bonding.
key_observables: Electrical conductivity, Seebeck coefficient, power factor (S^2sigma), lattice thermal conductivity, carrier mobility, and thermoelectric figure of merit (ZT).
important_parameters: Temperature of 300 K, carrier concentrations of 1e18 to 1e20 cm^-3, effective mass (m*), ionic dielectric constant (epsilon_ion), and Fröhlich coupling constant (alpha_po).
main_assumptions: Calculations of electron transport were performed within the constant relaxation time approximation for certain components; phonon transport was treated as diffusive (particle-like).
figures_summary: The figures include scattering rate decompositions (total, long-range, and remainder), phonon dispersion and density of states, thermal conductivity spectra, and analysis of the ionic dielectric constant and bonding indices.
paper_structure: The paper begins with a high-throughput screening of 236 compounds, followed by a detailed investigation of the physical mechanism of POP scattering suppression, a specific case study of GaGe2Te's electronic and phononic properties, and a concluding discussion on the design principles for decoupling transport.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Screening workﬂow for identifying layered semiconductors with high carrier mobility and power factor at room temperature.
Figure 2. Distribution of the 236 screened compounds in the parameter space deﬁned by the polar coupling constant αpo and conductivity mass m∗ c along the cross-plane direction.

Figure 3. (a) The cross-plane carrier mobility at 300 K and a carrier concentration of 1 × 1019 cm−3, calculated by considering both ADP and POP scattering, as a function of the product of αpo and m∗ c for materials that satisfy both screening thresholds. The symbol color represents the fraction of polar optical phonon scattering in the total scattering rate. (b) The correlation between the high-frequency dielectric constant ϵ∞and 1/ϵ∞−1/ϵ0. (c) The maximum power factor at room temperature among the high-mobility samples, considering three scattering mechanisms.

Figure 4. (a) The conventional unit cell and (b) the ELF of along [110] direction for GaGe2Te. The black arrows mark the chemical bond lengths and the vertical distances between layers, and the blue arrows are the corresponding integrated crystal orbital bond index ICOBI.

Figure 5. Element-resolved band structures, atom-projected density of states, enlarged views of the orbital-projected valence bands near the Γ point, -COHP curves calculated using the PBEsol exchangecorrelation functional, and Fermi surfaces of (a) GaGe2Te and (b) Mg3Sb2. The Fermi surfaces corresponding energy levels are indicated by blue dashed lines in the band-structure panels. The yellow and cyan surfaces represent the Fermi surfaces near the VBM and CBM, respectively.

Figure 6. Calculated electronic transport properties of GaGe2Te for p-type (left two columns) and n-type (right two columns) doping using the Perturbo package, including both electron–phonon and impurity (IMP) scattering: (a)–(d) electrical conductivity σ, (e)–(h) Seebeck coeﬃcient S, (i)–(l) electrical thermal conductivity κe, (m)–(p) power factor PF, and (q)–(t) ﬁgure of merit ZT, for carrier concentrations from 1018–1020 cm−3 and temperatures from 200 to 500 K.
Figure 7. (a) Total scattering rate (b) long-range and (c) remainder contributions for n-type and p-type doping.

Figure 8. (a) The atom resolution phonon dispersion including non-analytical corrections, phonon density of states PhDOS, lattice con- ductivity spectrum κ(ω), the ratio cumulative κL to total lattice thermal conductivity κt L and the 3ph and 4ph scattering rates (τ −1) at 300 K. (b) The calculated κL as a function of temperature in in-plane and cross-plane directions. (c) The phonon group velocity at room temperature in in-plane (left) and cross-plane (right). (d) The κ⊥/κ∥in diﬀerent compounds [35,71–74].
